1:
"Let as many servants as are under the yoke count their own masters worthy of all honour, that the name of God and his doctrine be not blasphemed." The witnesses note the charge that servants honour their masters for the gospel's sake. Gill notes the servants here are those "under the yoke" of government, in a state of servitude under the rule of men and in their service; these are to count their masters worthy of honour. The witnesses note Paul charging that servants under the yoke count their masters worthy of all honour, lest God's name and doctrine be blasphemed; the honour servants owe their masters to guard the gospel's good name — that Paul directs Christian slaves (very many of the early believers) in their hard lot: those "under the yoke" of servitude are to "count their own masters worthy of all honour" (yielding them due respect, reverence, and faithful service), and that with a weighty motive — "that the name of God and his doctrine be not blasphemed" — lest, by insolent or unfaithful servants, the gospel be reproached as breeding rebellion, the believer's lowly faithfulness being made to adorn the doctrine; count their own masters worthy of all honour. The witnesses note count their own masters worthy of all honour. The witnesses note that the name of God and his doctrine be not blasphemed.

The witnesses note the charge that servants honour their masters — lest God's name and doctrine be blasphemed; the believer's lowly faithfulness adorning the gospel.

So the witnesses make the verse the honour servants owe their masters. The lesson the expositors draw is that Christian servants are to honour their masters faithfully, lest the gospel be reproached. The witnesses note the under the yoke: "as many servants as are under the yoke" — Gill notes servants under the yoke of servitude; the under the yoke; the servitude; the the rule of men; the slaves; the service. The expositors observe the count worthy: "count their own masters worthy of all honour" — yielding them due respect and service; the count worthy; the all honour; the respect; the reverence; the service. The witnesses press the the name of God: "that the name of God... be not blasphemed" — lest God's name be blasphemed; the the name of God; the not blasphemed; the the gospel; the reproached; the dishonoured. The expositors draw the lesson on the his doctrine: "and his doctrine" — and his doctrine be not reproached; the his doctrine; the the teaching; the reproached; the the gospel; the dishonoured. The witnesses observe the not rebellion: lest the gospel be reproached as breeding rebellion; the not rebellion; the insolent; the unfaithful; the reproached; the the gospel. The expositors note the adorn the doctrine: the believer's lowly faithfulness adorns the doctrine; the adorn the doctrine; the faithfulness; the lowly; the the gospel; the commends. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the count their own masters worthy of all honour, the that the name of God and his doctrine be not blasphemed, and the honour owed. The verse thus records the honour servants owe their masters — teaching that Christian servants are to honour their masters faithfully, lest the gospel be reproached.
2:
"And they that have believing masters, let them not despise them, because they are brethren; but rather do them service, because they are faithful and beloved, partakers of the benefit. These things teach and exhort." The witnesses note the special duty of servants with believing masters. Chrysostom notes a teacher needs both authority and gentleness; Paul teaches both, here saying "these things teach and exhort," for a physician entreats the sick not for his own benefit but theirs. The witnesses note Paul charging that servants of believing masters not despise them because they are brethren, but serve them the more, since they are faithful and beloved; the servant's duty not to presume on Christian brotherhood — that Paul guards against a peculiar temptation: a servant whose master was also a Christian "brother" might be tempted to "despise" him, presuming on their spiritual equality to slacken his service; against this Paul charges that such "rather do them service" — serve all the more diligently — precisely "because they are faithful and beloved, partakers of the benefit" (fellow-sharers in grace), so that the bond of brotherhood is a reason for better, not worse, service; and he bids Timothy, "these things teach and exhort"; rather do them service, because they are faithful and beloved. The witnesses note let them not despise them, because they are brethren. The witnesses note but rather do them service, because they are faithful and beloved.

The witnesses note the servant's duty not to presume on Christian brotherhood — but to serve a believing master the more, since he is a faithful and beloved fellow-sharer in grace.

So the witnesses make the verse the duty toward believing masters. The lesson the expositors draw is that the bond of brotherhood is a reason for better, not slacker, service. The witnesses note the not despise: "let them not despise them, because they are brethren" — not to despise a believing master, presuming on brotherhood; the not despise; the believing master; the brethren; the presuming; the equality. The expositors observe the temptation: the servant might slacken service, presuming on spiritual equality; the the temptation; the slacken; the spiritual equality; the presuming; the despise. The witnesses press the do them service: "but rather do them service" — but serve them the more diligently; the do them service; the the more; the diligently; the serve; the better. The expositors draw the lesson on the faithful and beloved: "because they are faithful and beloved" — because they are faithful and beloved; the faithful and beloved; the fellow-sharers; the in grace; the brethren; the loved. The witnesses observe the partakers: "partakers of the benefit" — fellow-sharers in the benefit of grace; the partakers; the the benefit; the fellow-sharers; the grace; the shared. The expositors note the teach and exhort: "these things teach and exhort" — Chrysostom notes Paul bids Timothy teach and exhort; the teach and exhort; the Timothy; the teach; the exhort; the charge. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the let them not despise them because they are brethren, the but rather do them service because they are faithful and beloved, and the duty toward believing masters. The verse thus records the duty toward believing masters — teaching that the bond of brotherhood is a reason for better, not slacker, service.
3:
"If any man teach otherwise, and consent not to wholesome words, even the words of our Lord Jesus Christ, and to the doctrine which is according to godliness;" The witnesses note the mark of the false teacher who departs from sound words. Gill notes that to "teach otherwise" is to teach a doctrine different from what Paul had taught — as the false teachers did, who despised dominion, spoke evil of magistrates, and unsettled the order of church and household. The witnesses note Paul describing the man who teaches otherwise and consents not to the wholesome words of Christ and the doctrine of godliness; the test of teaching by the words of Christ and the doctrine of godliness — that Paul sets the standard against which every teacher is judged: the false teacher is one who "teach[es] otherwise" (a doctrine contrary to the apostolic gospel) and "consent[s] not to wholesome words" — that is, does not agree with the "sound, health-giving words, even the words of our Lord Jesus Christ" (Christ's own teaching), "and to the doctrine which is according to godliness" (the teaching that produces and accords with holy living) — so that the double test of truth is conformity to Christ's words and a godward, godliness-producing tendency; wholesome words, even the words of our Lord Jesus Christ. The witnesses note if any man teach otherwise, and consent not to wholesome words. The witnesses note the doctrine which is according to godliness.

The witnesses note the test of teaching — conformity to the wholesome words of Christ and the doctrine of godliness; the false teacher departs from both.

So the witnesses make the verse the test of teaching by Christ's words and godliness. The lesson the expositors draw is that the false teacher departs from Christ's sound words and from the doctrine that produces holiness. The witnesses note the teach otherwise: "if any man teach otherwise" — Gill notes teaching a doctrine different from the apostolic; the teach otherwise; the different doctrine; the contrary; the the apostolic; the false. The expositors observe the consent not: "and consent not to wholesome words" — does not agree with sound, health-giving words; the consent not; the wholesome words; the sound; the health-giving; the disagrees. The witnesses press the words of Christ: "even the words of our Lord Jesus Christ" — Christ's own teaching; the words of Christ; the Christ's teaching; the his own words; the sound; the the standard. The expositors draw the lesson on the godliness: "and to the doctrine which is according to godliness" — the teaching that produces holy living; the godliness; the holy living; the produces; the accords; the godward. The witnesses observe the double test: the double test is Christ's words and a godliness-producing tendency; the double test; the Christ's words; the godliness; the tendency; the truth. The expositors note the standard: every teacher is judged by this standard; the standard; the judged; the every teacher; the truth; the test. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the if any man teach otherwise and consent not to wholesome words, the the doctrine which is according to godliness, and the test of teaching. The verse thus records the test of teaching by Christ's words and godliness — teaching that the false teacher departs from Christ's sound words and from the doctrine that produces holiness.
4:
"He is proud, knowing nothing, but doting about questions and strifes of words, whereof cometh envy, strife, railings, evil surmisings," The witnesses note the character and fruits of the false teacher. Gill notes such a man "is proud," swelled and puffed up with a vain conceit of himself and his notions, treating the faithful ministers of the word with a haughty air; for the doctrines of grace are humbling, but notional knowledge puffs up. The witnesses note Paul declaring that such a man is proud, knowing nothing, but doting about questions and strifes of words, whence come envy, strife, railings, and evil surmisings; the pride and quarrelsome fruit of the false teacher's empty knowledge — that Paul paints the false teacher: for all his pretence, he "is proud" (puffed up with conceit), yet "knowing nothing" (truly ignorant of the saving truth), and "doting about questions and strifes of words" (morbidly obsessed with idle controversies and verbal disputes), and the harvest of this is wholly evil — "envy, strife, railings, evil surmisings" (jealousy, quarrelling, abusive speech, and suspicious thoughts) — so that the empty, contentious teaching breeds nothing but discord and corruption; doting about questions and strifes of words, whereof cometh envy, strife, railings. The witnesses note he is proud, knowing nothing, but doting about questions and strifes of words. The witnesses note whereof cometh envy, strife, railings, evil surmisings.

The witnesses note the proud, ignorant false teacher — doting about word-strifes — whose empty controversy breeds only envy, strife, railings, and evil surmisings.

So the witnesses make the verse the pride and quarrelsome fruit of false teaching. The lesson the expositors draw is that the false teacher, proud yet ignorant, breeds only discord by his empty disputes. The witnesses note the proud: "he is proud" — Gill notes puffed up with conceit, haughty; the proud; the puffed up; the conceit; the haughty; the vain. The expositors observe the knowing nothing: "knowing nothing" — truly ignorant of the saving truth; the knowing nothing; the ignorant; the the saving truth; the empty; the no knowledge. The witnesses press the doting: "but doting about questions and strifes of words" — morbidly obsessed with idle controversies and verbal disputes; the doting; the questions; the strifes of words; the controversies; the disputes. The expositors draw the lesson on the envy and strife: "whereof cometh envy, strife" — whence come envy and strife; the envy; the strife; the jealousy; the quarrelling; the discord. The witnesses observe the railings: "railings, evil surmisings" — abusive speech and suspicious thoughts; the railings; the evil surmisings; the abuse; the suspicion; the discord. The expositors note the breeds corruption: the empty teaching breeds only discord and corruption; the breeds corruption; the discord; the the harvest; the evil; the corruption. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the he is proud knowing nothing but doting about questions and strifes of words, the whereof cometh envy strife railings evil surmisings, and the fruit of false teaching. The verse thus records the pride and quarrelsome fruit of false teaching — teaching that the false teacher, proud yet ignorant, breeds only discord.
5:
"Perverse disputings of men of corrupt minds, and destitute of the truth, supposing that gain is godliness: from such withdraw thyself." The witnesses note the corrupt disputers who make religion a means of gain. Gill notes these are men corrupt in their principles, and corrupters of the word of God, who dispute in a froward and perverse way, rubbing and galling one another, provoking to wrath and every evil work. The witnesses note Paul describing perverse disputings of men of corrupt minds, destitute of truth, supposing gain to be godliness, and bidding Timothy withdraw from such; the false teachers who use religion for profit, and the charge to shun them — that Paul completes his portrait: these are "men of corrupt minds, and destitute of the truth" (their understanding depraved, and the truth gone from them), given to "perverse disputings," and worst of all "supposing that gain is godliness" — that is, treating religion as a means of worldly profit, valuing godliness only for what it can earn; and from "such," Paul charges, Timothy is to "withdraw" himself — to keep clear of these corrupt, mercenary teachers; from such withdraw thyself. The witnesses note men of corrupt minds, and destitute of the truth, supposing that gain is godliness. The witnesses note from such withdraw thyself.

The witnesses note the corrupt disputers who make gain their godliness — treating religion as a means of profit; the charge to withdraw from such mercenary teachers.

So the witnesses make the verse the false teachers who use religion for gain. The lesson the expositors draw is that men who treat godliness as a means of profit are to be shunned. The witnesses note the corrupt minds: "men of corrupt minds, and destitute of the truth" — Gill notes corrupt in principle, corrupters of the word; the corrupt minds; the destitute of truth; the depraved; the the truth gone; the corrupters. The expositors observe the perverse disputings: "perverse disputings" — given to froward, galling disputes; the perverse disputings; the froward; the galling; the disputes; the strife. The witnesses press the gain is godliness: "supposing that gain is godliness" — treating religion as a means of worldly profit; the gain is godliness; the worldly profit; the means; the religion; the mercenary. The expositors draw the lesson on the valuing godliness for gain: valuing godliness only for what it can earn; the valuing for gain; the what it earns; the profit; the mercenary; the godliness. The witnesses observe the withdraw: "from such withdraw thyself" — Timothy is to withdraw from such; the withdraw; the from such; the shun; the keep clear; the avoid. The expositors note the mercenary teachers: he is to keep clear of these corrupt, mercenary teachers; the mercenary teachers; the corrupt; the keep clear; the shun; the avoid. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the men of corrupt minds and destitute of the truth supposing that gain is godliness, the from such withdraw thyself, and the false teachers for gain. The verse thus records the false teachers who use religion for gain — teaching that men who treat godliness as a means of profit are to be shunned.
6:
"But godliness with contentment is great gain." The witnesses note the true gain — godliness joined to contentment. Gill notes by "godliness" is meant not any particular grace, but all the graces of the Spirit — faith, hope, love, fear — the whole of internal religion as it shows itself in worship and holy living. The witnesses note Paul declaring that godliness with contentment is great gain; the true riches of a godly, contented heart — that Paul, turning the false teachers' phrase, gives the truth: not that "gain is godliness," but that "godliness with contentment is great gain" — true religion, joined to a heart contented with what God provides, is itself the greatest of all riches; for while the mercenary seek gain by godliness and find only loss, the godly and contented soul possesses, in God and in a quiet, satisfied heart, a wealth that no money can buy and no loss can take away; godliness with contentment is great gain. The witnesses note godliness with contentment. The witnesses note is great gain.

The witnesses note the true gain — godliness with contentment; true religion joined to a heart satisfied with what God provides is itself the greatest of all riches.

So the witnesses make the verse the true riches of godliness and contentment. The lesson the expositors draw is that godliness joined to a contented heart is itself great gain, beyond all money. The witnesses note the godliness: "but godliness" — Gill notes godliness, all the graces of the Spirit, internal religion; the godliness; the the graces; the internal religion; the holy living; the worship. The expositors observe the contentment: "with contentment" — joined to a heart contented with what God provides; the contentment; the contented heart; the satisfied; the what God provides; the quiet. The witnesses press the great gain: "is great gain" — is the greatest of all riches; the great gain; the the greatest; the riches; the wealth; the gain. The expositors draw the lesson on the turning the phrase: Paul turns the false teachers' phrase to the truth; the turning the phrase; the the false teachers; the the truth; the not gain is godliness; the reversed. The witnesses observe the mercenary find loss: the mercenary seek gain by godliness and find loss; the mercenary find loss; the seek gain; the by godliness; the loss; the empty. The expositors note the wealth no money buys: the godly, contented soul has a wealth no money buys; the wealth no money buys; the the godly soul; the contented; the no loss takes; the riches. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the godliness with contentment, the is great gain, and the true riches. The verse thus records the true riches of godliness and contentment — teaching that godliness joined to a contented heart is itself great gain, beyond all money.
7:
"For we brought nothing into this world, and it is certain we can carry nothing out." The witnesses note the reason for contentment — we bring nothing in and take nothing out. Gill notes this is a reason both showing that godliness is great gain, since the godly brought nothing into the world but sin yet are now so happy, and that the godly should be content with what they have of worldly things. The witnesses note Paul reasoning that we brought nothing into this world and can carry nothing out; the transience of all earthly possession as the ground of contentment — that Paul gives the reason why the heart should be content: a man "brought nothing into this world" at his birth (he came naked and empty) and "it is certain we can carry nothing out" at his death (he leaves all behind, taking not a penny with him); since, then, all earthly goods are but a passing loan held for a brief span between two empty-handed states, it is folly to set the heart on them, and wisdom to be content with what God provides for the journey; we brought nothing into this world, and it is certain we can carry nothing out. The witnesses note we brought nothing into this world. The witnesses note it is certain we can carry nothing out.

The witnesses note the ground of contentment — we brought nothing in and can carry nothing out; earthly goods a passing loan between two empty-handed states.

So the witnesses make the verse the transience of possession as the ground of contentment. The lesson the expositors draw is that, since we bring nothing in and take nothing out, it is folly to set the heart on earthly goods. The witnesses note the brought nothing: "we brought nothing into this world" — Gill notes we came naked and empty into the world; the brought nothing; the naked; the empty; the at birth; the came. The expositors observe the carry nothing out: "and it is certain we can carry nothing out" — we leave all behind at death; the carry nothing out; the leave behind; the at death; the nothing; the empty. The witnesses press the passing loan: earthly goods are a passing loan held for a brief span; the passing loan; the brief span; the held; the loan; the temporary. The expositors draw the lesson on the two empty states: held between two empty-handed states (birth and death); the two empty states; the birth; the death; the empty-handed; the between. The witnesses observe the folly to set the heart: it is folly to set the heart on them; the folly; the set the heart; the on them; the earthly; the foolish. The expositors note the content with provision: wisdom is to be content with what God provides for the journey; the content with provision; the what God provides; the the journey; the content; the wise. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the we brought nothing into this world, the it is certain we can carry nothing out, and the ground of contentment. The verse thus records the transience of possession as the ground of contentment — teaching that, since we bring nothing in and take nothing out, it is folly to set the heart on earthly goods.
8:
"And having food and raiment let us be therewith content." The witnesses note the call to contentment with food and raiment. Gill notes "food" includes all things necessary to be eaten and drunk, of which there is a great variety, and "raiment" every necessary covering, including a habitation, a shelter from the weather; having these, we should be content. The witnesses note Paul charging that, having food and raiment, we be therewith content; the simple sufficiency that should satisfy the believer — that Paul draws from the transience of all things the lesson of contentment with little: "having food and raiment" — the plain necessaries of life, enough to eat and to wear (and, as Gill notes, a roof for shelter) — "let us be therewith content," not craving abundance or luxury, but resting satisfied when our real needs are supplied; for the believer who looks beyond this passing world finds in simple sufficiency, with God, all that he requires; having food and raiment let us be therewith content. The witnesses note having food and raiment. The witnesses note let us be therewith content.

The witnesses note the call to be content with food and raiment — the plain necessaries of life; the believer resting satisfied when his real needs are supplied, not craving abundance.

So the witnesses make the verse the contentment with simple sufficiency. The lesson the expositors draw is that the believer is to rest content when food and raiment are supplied, not craving luxury. The witnesses note the food: "having food" — Gill notes food, all necessary to eat and drink; the food; the necessary; the to eat; the to drink; the sustenance. The expositors observe the raiment: "and raiment" — and raiment, every necessary covering, including a shelter; the raiment; the covering; the clothing; the shelter; the habitation. The witnesses press the content: "let us be therewith content" — let us be content with these; the content; the therewith; the satisfied; the resting; the not craving. The expositors draw the lesson on the not luxury: not craving abundance or luxury; the not luxury; the not abundance; the not craving; the simple; the content. The witnesses observe the real needs: resting satisfied when real needs are supplied; the real needs; the supplied; the satisfied; the resting; the enough. The expositors note the all he requires: the believer finds in simple sufficiency, with God, all he requires; the all he requires; the simple sufficiency; the with God; the enough; the content. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the having food and raiment, the let us be therewith content, and the contentment with sufficiency. The verse thus records the contentment with simple sufficiency — teaching that the believer is to rest content when food and raiment are supplied, not craving luxury.
9:
"But they that will be rich fall into temptation and a snare, and into many foolish and hurtful lusts, which drown men in destruction and perdition." The witnesses note the perils that beset those determined to be rich. Gill notes it is not "they that are rich," for some rich men are good and do much good and are free from these snares — as Abraham, Joseph of Arimathea, Gaius — but "they that will be rich," who labour and make haste after it. The witnesses note Paul warning that they that will be rich fall into temptation, a snare, and many foolish and hurtful lusts which drown men in destruction; the ruinous snares of the craving to be rich — that Paul carefully marks the danger not in riches themselves but in the determined craving for them: "they that will be rich" (those whose set purpose and passion is to grow wealthy) "fall into temptation and a snare" (are trapped by the devil), "and into many foolish and hurtful lusts" (a host of senseless and harmful desires), which at last "drown men in destruction and perdition" — sinking them, as in deep water, into ruin both temporal and eternal; so that it is the love and pursuit of wealth, not its possession, that is so deadly; many foolish and hurtful lusts, which drown men in destruction and perdition. The witnesses note they that will be rich fall into temptation and a snare. The witnesses note which drown men in destruction and perdition.

The witnesses note the ruinous snares of the craving to be rich — not riches themselves but the determined pursuit, which traps men in lusts that drown them in destruction.

So the witnesses make the verse the perils of the craving for riches. The lesson the expositors draw is that the determined pursuit of wealth, not its possession, traps men in ruinous lusts. The witnesses note the will be rich: "they that will be rich" — Gill notes not the rich, but those determined to be rich; the will be rich; the determined; the craving; the the pursuit; the passion. The expositors observe the not the rich: it is not "they that are rich" — some rich are good and free; the not the rich; the some good; the free; the Abraham; the Joseph. The witnesses press the temptation and snare: "fall into temptation and a snare" — trapped by the devil; the temptation; the a snare; the trapped; the the devil; the caught. The expositors draw the lesson on the foolish lusts: "and into many foolish and hurtful lusts" — a host of senseless, harmful desires; the foolish lusts; the hurtful; the senseless; the harmful; the desires. The witnesses observe the drown: "which drown men in destruction and perdition" — which drown men in ruin; the drown; the destruction; the perdition; the ruin; the sinking. The expositors note the temporal and eternal: ruin both temporal and eternal; the temporal and eternal; the ruin; the destruction; the perdition; the both. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the they that will be rich fall into temptation and a snare, the which drown men in destruction and perdition, and the perils of craving riches. The verse thus records the perils of the craving for riches — teaching that the determined pursuit of wealth traps men in ruinous lusts.
10:
"For the love of money is the root of all evil: which while some coveted after, they have erred from the faith, and pierced themselves through with many sorrows." The witnesses note that the love of money, not money itself, is the root of all evil. Gill notes it is not money itself — silver and gold, God's creatures and gifts, which may be used to good purposes — but the love of it; and not any love, for there may be a lawful desire for it, but the inordinate love. The witnesses note Paul declaring that the love of money is the root of all evil, which some coveting have erred from the faith and pierced themselves with many sorrows; the inordinate love of money as the spring of evils and ruin — that Paul names the deep cause of those ruinous lusts: "the love of money" (covetousness, the inordinate craving for wealth, not money itself, which is God's good gift rightly used) "is the root of all evil" (or of all kinds of evil, the spring from which countless sins flow); and he shows its bitter fruit: "while some coveted after" it, "they have erred from the faith" (been seduced from the truth into apostasy) "and pierced themselves through with many sorrows" — wounding their own souls with self-inflicted griefs, so that covetousness is both spiritually and inwardly destructive; the love of money is the root of all evil. The witnesses note the love of money is the root of all evil. The witnesses note pierced themselves through with many sorrows.

The witnesses note the love of money — not money itself — as the root of all evil; some coveting it have erred from the faith and pierced themselves with many sorrows.

So the witnesses make the verse the inordinate love of money as the spring of evils. The lesson the expositors draw is that covetousness, not money itself, is the root of countless sins and self-inflicted sorrows. The witnesses note the love of money: "the love of money is the root of all evil" — Gill notes the love, not money itself; the love of money; the not money; the covetousness; the inordinate; the the craving. The expositors observe the not money itself: money is God's good gift, rightly used; the not money itself; the God's gift; the rightly used; the silver and gold; the good. The witnesses press the root of all evil: the love of money is the root of all (kinds of) evil; the root of all evil; the the spring; the all kinds; the countless sins; the the root. The expositors draw the lesson on the erred from the faith: "they have erred from the faith" — some, coveting, were seduced into apostasy; the erred from the faith; the seduced; the apostasy; the the truth; the strayed. The witnesses observe the pierced: "pierced themselves through with many sorrows" — wounding their own souls with self-inflicted griefs; the pierced; the many sorrows; the self-inflicted; the their souls; the wounded. The expositors note the destructive: covetousness is both spiritually and inwardly destructive; the destructive; the spiritually; the inwardly; the ruin; the sorrows. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the the love of money is the root of all evil, the pierced themselves through with many sorrows, and the love of money as the spring of evils. The verse thus records the inordinate love of money as the spring of evils — teaching that covetousness, not money itself, is the root of countless sins and self-inflicted sorrows.
11:
"But thou, O man of God, flee these things; and follow after righteousness, godliness, faith, love, patience, meekness." The witnesses note the charge to the man of God to flee and to follow. Gill notes Timothy is called "man of God" not merely as every man is by creation, nor only as every saint is by grace, but by his peculiar office as an evangelist and minister, qualified for, devoted to, and employed in God's service. The witnesses note Paul charging Timothy, as a man of God, to flee these things and follow after righteousness, godliness, faith, love, patience, and meekness; the double charge of the minister — to shun the love of money and pursue the graces — that Paul, turning from the covetous, addresses Timothy by the honourable title "O man of God" (a man belonging to and serving God), and lays on him a twofold charge: "flee these things" (the love of money and all its attendant evils, running from them as from a plague) and, on the other hand, "follow after" (earnestly pursue) the cluster of Christian graces — "righteousness, godliness, faith, love, patience, meekness" — the upright, godward, believing, loving, steadfast, and gentle spirit that should mark the man of God; flee these things; and follow after righteousness, godliness, faith, love, patience, meekness. The witnesses note O man of God, flee these things. The witnesses note follow after righteousness, godliness, faith, love, patience, meekness.

The witnesses note the double charge to the man of God — to flee the love of money and follow after righteousness, godliness, faith, love, patience, and meekness.

So the witnesses make the verse the double charge of the minister. The lesson the expositors draw is that the man of God is to flee covetousness and earnestly pursue the Christian graces. The witnesses note the man of God: "but thou, O man of God" — Gill notes Timothy called man of God by his office; the man of God; the the title; the his office; the the minister; the God's servant. The expositors observe the flee: "flee these things" — flee the love of money and its evils; the flee; the these things; the the love of money; the the evils; the run from. The witnesses press the follow after: "and follow after" — earnestly pursue; the follow after; the pursue; the earnestly; the seek; the chase. The expositors draw the lesson on the graces: "righteousness, godliness, faith, love, patience, meekness" — the cluster of Christian graces; the graces; the righteousness; the godliness; the faith and love; the patience and meekness. The witnesses observe the the spirit: the upright, godward, believing, loving, steadfast, gentle spirit; the the spirit; the upright; the godward; the loving; the gentle. The expositors note the should mark him: this should mark the man of God; the should mark him; the the man of God; the the minister; the the spirit; the graces. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the O man of God flee these things, the follow after righteousness godliness faith love patience meekness, and the double charge. The verse thus records the double charge of the minister — teaching that the man of God is to flee covetousness and pursue the Christian graces.
12:
"Fight the good fight of faith, lay hold on eternal life, whereunto thou art also called, and hast professed a good profession before many witnesses." The witnesses note the charge to fight the good fight and lay hold on eternal life. Gill notes Paul suggests that Timothy had other business than to mind the things of this world: his life was a warfare, he was a soldier, not to entangle himself with the things of this life, with many enemies to engage — Satan, sin, and the lusts of the flesh. The witnesses note Paul charging Timothy to fight the good fight of faith and lay hold on eternal life, to which he is called and which he had professed before many witnesses; the call to the spiritual warfare and the laying hold of life eternal — that Paul, in martial language, summons Timothy to his true work: "fight the good fight of faith" — wage the noble warfare of the Christian life and ministry, contending for and by the faith against all spiritual foes — and "lay hold on eternal life" (grasp and hold fast the life everlasting as the soldier's prize and goal), "whereunto thou art also called" (the very end of his calling) and to which he had "professed a good profession before many witnesses" (made open confession, perhaps at his baptism or ordination), a profession he must now make good; fight the good fight of faith, lay hold on eternal life. The witnesses note fight the good fight of faith, lay hold on eternal life. The witnesses note hast professed a good profession before many witnesses.

The witnesses note the call to fight the good fight of faith and lay hold on eternal life — the noble warfare of the Christian, making good the profession openly confessed.

So the witnesses make the verse the call to the spiritual warfare. The lesson the expositors draw is that Timothy is to wage the good fight of faith and grasp eternal life, the goal of his calling. The witnesses note the good fight: "fight the good fight of faith" — Gill notes the Christian life is a warfare, Timothy a soldier; the good fight; the warfare; the a soldier; the contending; the the faith. The expositors observe the foes: he has many enemies — Satan, sin, the lusts of the flesh; the the foes; the Satan; the sin; the the lusts; the enemies. The witnesses press the lay hold: "lay hold on eternal life" — grasp and hold fast eternal life as the prize; the lay hold; the eternal life; the the prize; the the goal; the grasp. The expositors draw the lesson on the called: "whereunto thou art also called" — to which he is called, the end of his calling; the called; the the end; the his calling; the the goal; the called. The witnesses observe the profession: "hast professed a good profession before many witnesses" — he had made open confession before many; the profession; the open confession; the before many; the witnesses; the confessed. The expositors note the make it good: a profession he must now make good; the make it good; the the profession; the now; the made good; the live up. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the fight the good fight of faith lay hold on eternal life, the hast professed a good profession before many witnesses, and the call to warfare. The verse thus records the call to the spiritual warfare — teaching that Timothy is to wage the good fight of faith and grasp eternal life, the goal of his calling.
13:
"I give thee charge in the sight of God, who quickeneth all things, and before Christ Jesus, who before Pontius Pilate witnessed a good confession;" The witnesses note the solemn charge given before God and the confessing Christ. Chrysostom notes Paul again calls God to witness, at once to increase his disciple's awe, to secure his safety, and to show these are not human commandments — that, receiving the charge as from the Lord himself and ever bearing in mind the Witness, Timothy may have it the more fearfully impressed on him. The witnesses note Paul giving Timothy charge in the sight of God who quickens all things, and before Christ Jesus who witnessed a good confession before Pontius Pilate; the solemn charge enforced by God and the faithful witness Christ — that Paul lays his closing charge with the utmost solemnity, before two great witnesses: "God, who quickeneth all things" (the living God who gives and preserves all life, and can raise Timothy if he die in the warfare), and "Christ Jesus, who before Pontius Pilate witnessed a good confession" (who, on trial for his life, fearlessly confessed the truth and his kingship) — so that Timothy, charged before the life-giving God and set the example of the faithful Confessor, might be moved to bold and faithful endurance; before Christ Jesus, who before Pontius Pilate witnessed a good confession. The witnesses note I give thee charge in the sight of God, who quickeneth all things. The witnesses note Christ Jesus, who before Pontius Pilate witnessed a good confession.

The witnesses note the charge before God who quickens all things and Christ who confessed before Pilate — Timothy set the example of the faithful Confessor for bold endurance.

So the witnesses make the verse the solemn charge before God and the confessing Christ. The lesson the expositors draw is that Timothy is charged before the life-giving God and the faithful Confessor Christ to bold endurance. The witnesses note the charge: "I give thee charge in the sight of God" — Chrysostom notes Paul calls God to witness; the charge; the in the sight of God; the witness; the solemn; the awe. The expositors observe the quickeneth all things: "who quickeneth all things" — the living God who gives and preserves all life; the quickeneth; the all things; the the living God; the gives life; the can raise. The witnesses press the Christ confessed: "and before Christ Jesus, who before Pontius Pilate witnessed a good confession" — Christ, on trial, confessed the truth; the Christ confessed; the before Pilate; the the truth; the his kingship; the fearless. The expositors draw the lesson on the faithful witness: Christ is the example of the faithful Confessor; the faithful witness; the the example; the Confessor; the fearless; the confessed. The witnesses observe the not human: Chrysostom notes these are not human commandments, but from the Lord; the not human; the from the Lord; the commandments; the awe; the impressed. The expositors note the bold endurance: Timothy is moved to bold and faithful endurance; the bold endurance; the faithful; the moved; the example; the endure. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the I give thee charge in the sight of God who quickeneth all things, the Christ Jesus who before Pontius Pilate witnessed a good confession, and the solemn charge. The verse thus records the solemn charge before God and the confessing Christ — teaching that Timothy is charged before the life-giving God and the faithful Confessor to bold endurance.
14:
"That thou keep this commandment without spot, unrebukeable, until the appearing of our Lord Jesus Christ:" The witnesses note the charge to keep the commandment blameless until Christ's appearing. Gill notes "this commandment" may mean the charge last given to fight the good fight, or the whole of the orders given throughout the epistle for the doctrine and discipline of God's house, or rather the work and office of preaching the gospel committed to him. The witnesses note Paul charging Timothy to keep this commandment without spot and unrebukeable until the appearing of the Lord Jesus Christ; the call to a blameless, faithful keeping of the trust to the very end — that Paul presses the charge home with its scope: Timothy is to "keep this commandment" (the whole trust of his ministry and the faith) "without spot" (unstained by error or sin) and "unrebukeable" (so that no just blame can be laid on him), and that not for a season only but "until the appearing of our Lord Jesus Christ" — to the very end, with the great day of Christ's return ever in view as the term of his faithfulness and the time of his reckoning; keep this commandment without spot, unrebukeable, until the appearing of our Lord Jesus Christ. The witnesses note keep this commandment without spot, unrebukeable. The witnesses note until the appearing of our Lord Jesus Christ.

The witnesses note the charge to keep the commandment blameless until Christ's appearing — the trust kept unstained and unrebukeable to the very end, with the great day in view.

So the witnesses make the verse the blameless keeping of the trust to the end. The lesson the expositors draw is that Timothy is to keep his ministry and the faith unstained and unrebukeable until Christ's appearing. The witnesses note the commandment: "that thou keep this commandment" — Gill notes the whole trust of his ministry and the faith; the commandment; the the trust; the the ministry; the the faith; the keep. The expositors observe the without spot: "without spot" — unstained by error or sin; the without spot; the unstained; the no error; the no sin; the clean. The witnesses press the unrebukeable: "unrebukeable" — so that no just blame can be laid; the unrebukeable; the no blame; the blameless; the no charge; the clean. The expositors draw the lesson on the until the appearing: "until the appearing of our Lord Jesus Christ" — to the very end, until Christ's return; the until the appearing; the the very end; the Christ's return; the the great day; the to the end. The witnesses observe the the day in view: the great day is ever in view as the term of faithfulness; the the day in view; the the great day; the the term; the the reckoning; the kept. The expositors note the not a season: not for a season only but to the end; the not a season; the to the end; the faithful; the persevere; the kept. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the keep this commandment without spot unrebukeable, the until the appearing of our Lord Jesus Christ, and the blameless keeping. The verse thus records the blameless keeping of the trust to the end — teaching that Timothy is to keep his ministry and the faith unstained until Christ's appearing.
15:
"Which in his times he shall shew, who is the blessed and only Potentate, the King of kings, and Lord of lords;" The witnesses note the doxology to God who shall show Christ's appearing in his time. Gill notes that though the time of Christ's appearing is unknown, the thing itself is certain; God will bring it about and make it manifest in his own time, the time fixed and appointed by him, known only to him and kept in his own power. The witnesses note Paul declaring that God in his times shall show Christ's appearing, being the blessed and only Potentate, the King of kings and Lord of lords; the majestic titles of the God who governs the times of Christ's coming — that Paul, having named the appearing of Christ, breaks into adoration of the God who appoints its season: "in his times he shall shew" it (in God's own fixed and appointed time), and Paul heaps up the titles of his sovereign majesty — "the blessed and only Potentate" (the supremely happy and sole Sovereign), "the King of kings, and Lord of lords" (ruling over all earthly rulers, infinitely above them all) — so that the timing of the great day rests wholly in the hand of the all-governing, all-blessed God; the blessed and only Potentate, the King of kings, and Lord of lords. The witnesses note which in his times he shall shew. The witnesses note the blessed and only Potentate, the King of kings, and Lord of lords.

The witnesses note the doxology — God in his own time shall show Christ's appearing, the blessed and only Potentate, King of kings and Lord of lords, who governs the great day.

So the witnesses make the verse the majesty of the God who governs the times. The lesson the expositors draw is that the timing of Christ's appearing rests wholly with the all-sovereign, all-blessed God. The witnesses note the in his times: "which in his times he shall shew" — Gill notes God shows it in his own fixed, appointed time; the in his times; the his own time; the appointed; the fixed; the known only to him. The expositors observe the certain: though the time is unknown, the thing is certain; the certain; the unknown time; the the thing; the manifest; the sure. The witnesses press the only Potentate: "the blessed and only Potentate" — the supremely happy and sole Sovereign; the only Potentate; the blessed; the sole Sovereign; the supreme; the happy. The expositors draw the lesson on the King of kings: "the King of kings, and Lord of lords" — ruling over all earthly rulers, above them all; the King of kings; the Lord of lords; the over all rulers; the above; the supreme. The witnesses observe the the timing rests with God: the timing of the great day rests wholly with God; the the timing; the the great day; the with God; the his hand; the governs. The expositors note the all-governing God: the all-governing, all-blessed God appoints the season; the all-governing; the all-blessed; the appoints; the the season; the God. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the which in his times he shall shew, the the blessed and only Potentate the King of kings and Lord of lords, and the majesty of God. The verse thus records the majesty of the God who governs the times — teaching that the timing of Christ's appearing rests wholly with the all-sovereign, all-blessed God.
16:
"Who only hath immortality, dwelling in the light which no man can approach unto; whom no man hath seen, nor can see: to whom be honour and power everlasting. Amen." The witnesses note the doxology to the immortal God dwelling in unapproachable light. Gill notes that angels are immortal, and so are the souls of men, and the bodies of men after the resurrection; but none of these have immortality of themselves — they have it from God, who only has it of himself, originally, essentially, and underivedly. The witnesses note Paul ascribing honour and power everlasting to the God who only has immortality, dwelling in unapproachable light, whom no man has seen or can see; the incomparable glory of the invisible, immortal God — that Paul completes the great ascription with the highest things that can be said of God: he "only hath immortality" (deathlessness underived and essential, his alone by nature, while all other immortals have it as his gift), "dwelling in the light which no man can approach unto" (in a blaze of glory no creature can come near), "whom no man hath seen, nor can see" (invisible to mortal eye in his pure essence) — and to this incomparable God Paul ascribes "honour and power everlasting," sealing the doxology with "Amen"; to whom be honour and power everlasting. Amen. The witnesses note who only hath immortality, dwelling in the light which no man can approach unto. The witnesses note to whom be honour and power everlasting. Amen.

The witnesses note the doxology to the immortal God — who alone has underived immortality, dwells in unapproachable light, unseen by mortal eye; to him honour and power everlasting.

So the witnesses make the verse the incomparable glory of the immortal God. The lesson the expositors draw is that God alone has essential immortality and dwells in unapproachable light, unseen, worthy of everlasting honour. The witnesses note the only immortality: "who only hath immortality" — Gill notes God only has immortality of himself, underived; the only immortality; the underived; the essential; the his alone; the of himself. The expositors observe the others have it from him: angels and souls are immortal, but have it as his gift; the others from him; the angels; the souls; the his gift; the derived. The witnesses press the unapproachable light: "dwelling in the light which no man can approach unto" — in a blaze of glory no creature can near; the unapproachable light; the the glory; the no creature; the come near; the blaze. The expositors draw the lesson on the unseen: "whom no man hath seen, nor can see" — invisible to mortal eye in his essence; the unseen; the no man hath seen; the invisible; the his essence; the cannot see. The witnesses observe the honour and power: "to whom be honour and power everlasting" — to him honour and power everlasting; the honour and power; the everlasting; the ascribed; the praise; the glory. The expositors note the incomparable God: this is the incomparable, invisible, immortal God; the incomparable God; the invisible; the immortal; the supreme; the glory. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the who only hath immortality dwelling in the light which no man can approach unto, the to whom be honour and power everlasting Amen, and the glory of God. The verse thus records the incomparable glory of the immortal God — teaching that God alone has essential immortality and dwells in unapproachable light, worthy of everlasting honour.
17:
"Charge them that are rich in this world, that they be not highminded, nor trust in uncertain riches, but in the living God, who giveth us richly all things to enjoy;" The witnesses note the charge to the rich not to be proud nor trust in wealth. Gill notes some are poor in this world but rich in another, others rich here but poor and miserable in the world to come, and some rich in both; Paul charges them that are rich in the things of this world. The witnesses note Paul charging the rich not to be highminded nor to trust in uncertain riches, but in the living God who gives all things richly to enjoy; the right use of wealth — humble trust in God, not in riches — that Paul, returning to the rich (those who already have wealth, as distinct from the craving "would-be rich" of v.9), gives them their charge: "that they be not highminded" (not proud and lifted up by their wealth), "nor trust in uncertain riches" (not lean on so fickle and fleeting a thing) "but in the living God" (the sure and abiding object of trust), "who giveth us richly all things to enjoy" — for it is God, not their riches, who is the giver of every good thing, and even of the power to enjoy it; so that the rich are to hold their wealth with humility and to trust the Giver, not the gift; nor trust in uncertain riches, but in the living God. The witnesses note that they be not highminded, nor trust in uncertain riches. The witnesses note but in the living God, who giveth us richly all things to enjoy.

The witnesses note the charge to the rich — not highminded, not trusting in uncertain riches, but in the living God who gives all things to enjoy; trusting the Giver, not the gift.

So the witnesses make the verse the right use of wealth in humble trust. The lesson the expositors draw is that the rich are to hold their wealth humbly and trust the living God, not their fleeting riches. The witnesses note the rich: "charge them that are rich in this world" — Gill notes the rich in the things of this world; the the rich; the in this world; the wealthy; the already rich; the the rich. The expositors observe the not highminded: "that they be not highminded" — not proud, lifted up by wealth; the not highminded; the not proud; the not lifted up; the humble; the lowly. The witnesses press the not trust riches: "nor trust in uncertain riches" — not lean on fickle, fleeting riches; the not trust riches; the uncertain; the fickle; the fleeting; the not lean. The expositors draw the lesson on the trust God: "but in the living God" — but trust the living God, the sure object; the trust God; the the living God; the sure; the abiding; the the object. The witnesses observe the giver of all: "who giveth us richly all things to enjoy" — God gives all things richly to enjoy; the giver of all; the richly; the all things; the to enjoy; the the Giver. The expositors note the Giver not the gift: the rich are to trust the Giver, not the gift; the Giver not the gift; the the Giver; the not the gift; the God; the trust. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the that they be not highminded nor trust in uncertain riches, the but in the living God who giveth us richly all things to enjoy, and the right use of wealth. The verse thus records the right use of wealth in humble trust — teaching that the rich are to hold their wealth humbly and trust the living God, not their fleeting riches.
18:
"That they do good, that they be rich in good works, ready to distribute, willing to communicate;" The witnesses note the charge that the rich be rich in good works and generous. Gill notes rich men sometimes do much ill, to their own bodies and souls and to others by fraud and oppression; for it is in their power to do much evil, and also much good, and much is required of them. The witnesses note Paul charging that the rich do good, be rich in good works, ready to distribute, and willing to communicate; the charge to turn wealth to generous good works — that Paul tells the rich how to use their abundance well: "that they do good" (employ their means in beneficence), "that they be rich in good works" (let their true riches be deeds of charity, not hoarded gold), "ready to distribute" (free and prompt to give to the needy), "willing to communicate" (glad to share their goods with others) — so that the wealthy are to become wealthy in a better currency, laying out their money in liberality and love; that they be rich in good works, ready to distribute, willing to communicate. The witnesses note that they do good, that they be rich in good works. The witnesses note ready to distribute, willing to communicate.

The witnesses note the charge to the rich — to do good, be rich in good works, ready to distribute and share; their true riches deeds of charity, not hoarded gold.

So the witnesses make the verse the charge to generous good works. The lesson the expositors draw is that the rich are to turn their wealth into beneficence, rich in good works and ready to give. The witnesses note the do good: "that they do good" — Gill notes employing their means in beneficence; the do good; the beneficence; the their means; the good; the use. The expositors observe the much good or ill: the rich can do much ill or much good; the much good or ill; the the rich; the the power; the much; the good. The witnesses press the rich in good works: "that they be rich in good works" — their true riches deeds of charity; the rich in good works; the true riches; the charity; the deeds; the not hoarded. The expositors draw the lesson on the ready to distribute: "ready to distribute" — free and prompt to give to the needy; the ready to distribute; the free; the prompt; the the needy; the give. The witnesses observe the willing to communicate: "willing to communicate" — glad to share their goods; the willing to communicate; the glad; the share; the their goods; the others. The expositors note the better currency: they become wealthy in a better currency, liberality and love; the better currency; the wealthy; the liberality; the love; the laid out. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the that they do good that they be rich in good works, the ready to distribute willing to communicate, and the charge to generosity. The verse thus records the charge to generous good works — teaching that the rich are to turn their wealth into beneficence, rich in good works.
19:
"Laying up in store for themselves a good foundation against the time to come, that they may lay hold on eternal life." The witnesses note the laying up of a heavenly treasure by good works. Gill notes that by such good works they are laying up a treasure in heaven, which will be for themselves to enjoy to all eternity; whereas what they lay up here is for others, for children or friends, and sometimes for strangers or even enemies. The witnesses note Paul declaring that the rich, by good works, lay up in store a good foundation against the time to come, that they may lay hold on eternal life; the heavenly treasure secured by a generous, godly use of wealth — that Paul gives the rich the great reward of such liberality: in distributing and doing good, they are "laying up in store for themselves a good foundation against the time to come" — securing, not by merit but by God's gracious appointment, a sure and lasting good laid up for the future, in heaven — "that they may lay hold on eternal life," that their godly use of wealth might be bound up with the grasping of life everlasting; so that money rightly used becomes a means of laying treasure in heaven; laying up in store for themselves a good foundation against the time to come. The witnesses note laying up in store for themselves a good foundation against the time to come. The witnesses note that they may lay hold on eternal life.

The witnesses note the heavenly treasure secured by generous good works — a good foundation laid up against the time to come, that the rich may lay hold on eternal life.

So the witnesses make the verse the heavenly treasure of generous wealth. The lesson the expositors draw is that the rich, by good works, lay up a sure foundation in heaven, that they may lay hold on eternal life. The witnesses note the laying up: "laying up in store for themselves a good foundation" — Gill notes laying up a treasure in heaven for themselves; the laying up; the in store; the a treasure; the in heaven; the for themselves. The expositors observe the for themselves: this treasure is for themselves to enjoy eternally, unlike earthly wealth left to others; the for themselves; the eternally; the unlike earthly; the left to others; the their own. The witnesses press the against the time to come: "against the time to come" — a sure good laid up for the future; the against the time to come; the the future; the laid up; the sure; the lasting. The expositors draw the lesson on the not merit: not by merit but by God's gracious appointment; the not merit; the God's appointment; the gracious; the not earned; the by grace. The witnesses observe the lay hold on life: "that they may lay hold on eternal life" — that they may grasp eternal life; the lay hold on life; the eternal life; the grasp; the bound up; the the reward. The expositors note the treasure in heaven: money rightly used lays treasure in heaven; the treasure in heaven; the money; the rightly used; the laid up; the heaven. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the laying up in store for themselves a good foundation against the time to come, the that they may lay hold on eternal life, and the heavenly treasure. The verse thus records the heavenly treasure of generous wealth — teaching that the rich, by good works, lay up a sure foundation in heaven.
20:
"O Timothy, keep that which is committed to thy trust, avoiding profane and vain babblings, and oppositions of science falsely so called:" The witnesses note the closing charge to guard the trust and shun false knowledge. Gill notes "that which is committed to thy trust" is the gospel, a rich treasure put into earthen vessels, to be kept pure and uncorrupt, faithfully dispensed and diligently preserved, that it be neither adulterated nor taken away by false teachers. The witnesses note Paul charging Timothy to keep the trust committed to him, avoiding profane and vain babblings and the oppositions of science falsely so called; the guarding of the deposit against empty and false knowledge — that Paul, with a personal and earnest "O Timothy," lays the climactic charge: "keep that which is committed to thy trust" — guard the precious deposit of the gospel entrusted to him — and to that end shun two things: "profane and vain babblings" (godless, empty talk) and "oppositions of science falsely so called" (the contradictions of a "knowledge" that wrongly bears the name, the pretended higher wisdom of the false teachers, which is no true knowledge at all) — so that fidelity to the gospel requires both holding it fast and turning from all that counterfeits it; keep that which is committed to thy trust. The witnesses note keep that which is committed to thy trust. The witnesses note avoiding profane and vain babblings, and oppositions of science falsely so called.

The witnesses note the climactic charge — keep the trust, the precious deposit of the gospel, avoiding empty babbling and the contradictions of "science falsely so called."

So the witnesses make the verse the guarding of the gospel deposit. The lesson the expositors draw is that Timothy is to keep the trust of the gospel, shunning empty talk and false, counterfeit knowledge. The witnesses note the keep the trust: "keep that which is committed to thy trust" — Gill notes the gospel, a treasure to be kept pure; the keep the trust; the the gospel; the the deposit; the kept pure; the guarded. The expositors observe the earthen vessels: the gospel is a rich treasure in earthen vessels; the earthen vessels; the the treasure; the rich; the dispensed; the preserved. The witnesses press the vain babblings: "avoiding profane and vain babblings" — shun godless, empty talk; the vain babblings; the profane; the empty talk; the godless; the shun. The expositors draw the lesson on the science falsely so called: "and oppositions of science falsely so called" — the contradictions of a knowledge wrongly named; the science falsely so called; the wrongly named; the the contradictions; the false knowledge; the counterfeit. The witnesses observe the pretended wisdom: the pretended higher wisdom of the false teachers is no true knowledge; the pretended wisdom; the the false teachers; the no true knowledge; the counterfeit; the false. The expositors note the hold fast and turn: fidelity requires holding the gospel fast and turning from counterfeits; the hold fast and turn; the holding fast; the turning from; the counterfeits; the fidelity. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the keep that which is committed to thy trust, the avoiding profane and vain babblings and oppositions of science falsely so called, and the guarding of the deposit. The verse thus records the guarding of the gospel deposit — teaching that Timothy is to keep the trust, shunning empty talk and counterfeit knowledge.
21:
"Which some professing have erred concerning the faith. Grace be with thee. Amen." The witnesses note the warning of those who erred by such false knowledge, and the closing benediction. Gill notes "some professing" pretended to be masters of the above "science," boasting and making a great show of it, and valuing themselves upon it. Barnes notes that some who professed to be true Christians, attracted by false philosophy, were led to deny the doctrines of Christianity. The witnesses note Paul observing that some, professing this false knowledge, have erred concerning the faith, and closing with the benediction of grace; the ruin of those who embraced false knowledge, and the parting blessing — that Paul, with a final warning, points to the sad effect of the "science falsely so called": "some professing" it (claiming and parading this counterfeit wisdom) "have erred concerning the faith" — wandered from the true gospel into error and even apostasy — a solemn proof of the danger Timothy was charged to avoid; and then, in the briefest of closes, Paul ends the epistle with his accustomed benediction, "Grace be with thee. Amen" — invoking God's free favour upon Timothy; which some professing have erred concerning the faith. The witnesses note which some professing have erred concerning the faith. The witnesses note Grace be with thee. Amen.

The witnesses note the ruin of those who professed false knowledge — they erred concerning the faith — and the parting blessing: "Grace be with thee. Amen."

So the witnesses make the verse the warning and closing benediction. The lesson the expositors draw is that those who embraced false knowledge erred from the faith, a warning sealed with the blessing of grace. The witnesses note the professing: "which some professing" — Gill notes some pretended to master this false science; the professing; the pretended; the the false science; the boasting; the paraded. The expositors observe Barnes's note: Barnes notes some professing Christians, drawn by false philosophy, denied the doctrines; the professing Christians; the false philosophy; the drawn; the denied; the the doctrines. The witnesses press the erred: "have erred concerning the faith" — wandered from the true gospel into error; the erred; the the faith; the wandered; the error; the apostasy. The expositors draw the lesson on the danger: a solemn proof of the danger Timothy was to avoid; the the danger; the proof; the to avoid; the the warning; the solemn. The witnesses observe the benediction: "Grace be with thee. Amen" — Paul ends with the blessing of grace; the benediction; the grace; the be with thee; the the blessing; the close. The expositors note the parting blessing: invoking God's free favour upon Timothy; the parting blessing; the God's favour; the Timothy; the grace; the close. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the which some professing have erred concerning the faith, the Grace be with thee Amen, and the warning and blessing. The verse thus records the warning and closing benediction — teaching that those who embraced false knowledge erred from the faith, a warning sealed with the blessing of grace.
